Of the world’s roughly 12,000 warheads in the nine nuclear-armed states, as many as 2,200 were kept on high operational alert as of January this year, an increase of up to 100 from the previous year, per SIPRI.
China now has around 620 nuclear warheads and is expanding its arsenal faster than any other country.
The SIPRI report comes in the wake of Oslo’s announcement last week that it has opened discussions with Paris about France’s extended nuclear deterrence initiative, makes it the latest European capital to look to an alternative to its longtime strategic deterrent strategy — and likely not the last.
“Together with some of our closest partners and Allies, Norway will be discussing in more detail how France’s nuclear weapons can further enhance European security and deterrence,” Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Støre said.
The two leaders agreed that Finland will receive more detailed information soon, and then decide if Helsinki will join the pact, according to local media.
A former NATO Director of Arms Control, William Alberque, assessed that additional European countries are likely to join the talks with Macron as Paris’s nuclear deterrence has become clearer.
In March 2026, President Macron announced plans to increase France’s number of nuclear warheads.
The SIPRI report states that France has increased its nuclear warhead inventory by 80 in a single year, from 290 in January 2025 to 370 in January 2026."},{"url":"https://www.defensenews.com/global/europe/2026/06/01/norway-becomes-ninth-country-to-sign-up-for-french-nuclear-deterrence-as-trust-in-us-falters/","title":"Norway becomes ninth country to sign up for French nuclear deterrence as trust in US falters","content":"President Emmanuel Macron’s initiative is gaining steam, as German officials plan to observe French nuclear operations.
The announcement came following a visit by the Norwegian prime minister, Jonas Gahr Stoere, to Paris last week.
But the new French doctrine, which was announced in theatrical fashion by Emmanuel Macron, the country’s president, while standing in front of a nuclear submarine in March, promises to link existential threats to European allies to a French nuclear response even if the U.S. may disengage.
At around 290 warheads, the French nuclear arsenal is the fourth-largest in the world, after China, the U.S. and Russia, and ahead of the U.K."}]}
